response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"You gotta treat social media like a construction site, man—it looks grand from far away, but if you don't check the foundation, everything is gonna fall apart when it gets real pressure. Dealing with all this fake stuff online? It’s exhausting, honestly. When I see people getting worked up over some half-baked post or some deepfake video that ain't even true, I just kinda feel drained. My head starts spinning trying to figure out what's solid and what's just plywood nailed together for show. Since I spend my time building things with my hands—cutting lumber, fitting joints—I know the difference between something sturdy and something flimsy, and that sense of tactile reality is something these screens can never replicate. Fake content online feels like trying to nail a beam into wet sand; it's going nowhere reliable. The most important thing I've learned is that you gotta build some mental walls for yourself before you even log on. Don't just let whatever pops up in your feed dictate how you feel or what you believe. You need to be seriously conscientious about the sources, like checking a blueprint twice before starting a cut. If something sounds too crazy, or if it makes you instantly angry without giving you any real evidence, chances are it’s smoke and mirrors. I try to remember that not every opinion posted online is vetted by reality; they're just whatever people want to throw out there when they feel emotional. It takes a disciplined effort—a kind of mental carpentry—to sift through the noise. Maybe limit how often you look at these things, too. Just logging in sporadically, like I do now, keeps me from getting sucked into that endless cycle of outrage and misinformation. Focus on what's real, what's useful, or what genuinely connects you to something solid, whether that's a good piece of work or actual time outside with my garden. Don't let the digital clutter mess up your head like it messes up a straight board if you don't hold it right.","reasoning_summary":"The persona is a 30-year-old carpenter who is an immigrant, depressed (psychological feature), and has low agreeableness/low openness but high conscientiousness. The tone should be world-weary, highly pragmatic, slightly cynical, and grounded. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: You gotta treat social media like a construction site, man—it looks grand from far away, but if you don't check the foundation, everything is gonna fall apart when it gets real pressure. Dealing with all this fake stuff online? It’s exhausting, honestly. When I see people getting worked up over some half-baked post or some deepfake video that ain't even true, I just kinda feel drained. My head starts spinning trying to figure out what's solid and what's just plywood nailed together for show. Since I spend my time building things with my hands—cutting lumber, fitting joints—I know the difference between something sturdy and something flimsy, and that sense of tactile reality is something these screens can never replicate. Fake content online feels like trying to nail a beam into wet sand; it's going nowhere reliable. The most important thing I've learned is that you gotta build some mental walls for yourself before you even log on. Don't just let whatever pops up in your feed dictate how you feel or what you believe. You need to be seriously conscientious about the sources, like checking a blueprint twice before starting a cut. If something sounds too crazy, or if it makes you instantly angry without giving you any real evidence, chances are it’s smoke and mirrors. I try to remember that not every opinion posted online is vetted by reality; they're just whatever people want to throw out there when they feel emotional. It takes a disciplined effort—a kind of mental carpentry—to sift through the noise. Maybe limit how often you look at these things, too. Just logging in sporadically, like I do now, keeps me from getting sucked into that endless cycle of outrage and misinformation. Focus on what's real, what's useful, or what genuinely connects you to something solid, whether that's a good piece of work or actual time outside with my garden. Don't let the digital clutter mess up your head like it messes up a straight board if you don't hold it right.

The tone should be world-weary, highly pragmatic, slightly cynical, and grounded. I need to ensure the language reflects a practical, hands-on worker's perspective while maintaining enough depth to discuss media literacy effectively.
